planting.note: Plant in spring after the last frost in a location with good air circulation. The cultivar 'Profusion' is especially valued for its dense clusters of vivid violet berries.

directions: Set transplants in well-drained soil amended with compost, spacing them 4 to 6 feet apart to allow the arching branches to develop freely. Water regularly through the first growing season to establish deep roots, then reduce irrigation as the shrub becomes more drought tolerant. Cut stems back by about one-third in late winter to encourage vigorous new growth and the most prolific berry production the following autumn. The metallic violet-purple berry clusters persist on bare stems well into winter and are a standout in the landscape.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in acidic, well-drained soil. Cultivar selection matters greatly—dwarf types top out at 3 feet while standard forms can reach 10 feet or more, so choose based on available space.

directions: Plant in acidic, well-drained soil with a pH between 5.0 and 6.5, incorporating sulfur or acidifying fertilizer if your native soil is alkaline. Burgundy loropetalum establishes readily in Gulf Coast landscapes and rewards minimal pruning with its best natural form—avoid heavy shearing, which reduces flowering and distorts the graceful branching habit. Magenta-pink fringe flowers appear most heavily in late winter and spring, but sporadic bloom continues throughout the warm months. Mulch the root zone to conserve moisture and maintain the soil acidity this plant prefers.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sheltered spot with morning sun and afternoon shade in hotter inland zones. This slow-growing member of the tea family resents root disturbance, so site it carefully from the outset.

directions: Grow in moist, acidic, humus-rich soil similar to what camellias prefer, keeping the pH between 5.0 and 6.5 and the root zone consistently moist but never waterlogged. The large, white, camellia-like flowers with showy yellow stamens open from autumn through winter, providing color when little else is blooming in the garden. Protect young plants from hard freezes with frost cloth, as established specimens tolerate brief dips to the upper teens but young transplants are more vulnerable. Minimal pruning is needed; remove only crossing or damaged branches in late spring after the main flush of bloom.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a spot that receives morning sun and some afternoon shade in zone 9 summers. This Chinese species tolerates drier conditions than its American cousin Itea virginica, making it a versatile choice for Gulf Coast gardens.

directions: Plant in well-drained to moderately moist soil and water regularly until established, after which the shrub shows good drought tolerance compared to other sweetspires. Long, arching racemes of fragrant white flowers appear in early summer, followed by excellent red to orange-red fall foliage color that holds late into the season. Prune lightly after flowering to maintain a tidy shape and encourage branching, removing any dead or crowded stems at the base. Mulch annually to protect the root zone and retain soil moisture through the hottest months.

planting.note: Plant after all danger of frost has passed in zone 9; in frost-free areas it can be planted any time. This Honduran native thrives in the heat and humidity of Gulf Coast summers and will return reliably from the roots in mild winters.

directions: Plant in full sun in rich, well-drained soil and water consistently through the establishment period, then maintain moderate irrigation as the plant is sensitive to prolonged drought but equally intolerant of waterlogged roots. The enormous, velvety leaves emit a sweet peach fragrance when brushed, and tall spikes of bright magenta-pink flowers appear in late fall and winter, drawing hummingbirds throughout the cool season. Pinch stem tips during the growing season to promote bushy, compact growth and delay flowering until the plant is well branched. In zone 9, cut back frost-damaged stems to the ground in late winter and the plant will resprout vigorously from established roots.
